title: Interaction-enabled metal-insulator phase transition in a driven quantum gas
authors: Camilo Cantillano, Karthick Ramanathan, Zekai Chen, Ang Yang, Emilio Aguilera-Valdes, Lei Ying, Manuele Landini, Hanns-Christoph Nägerl, Yanliang Guo
abstract: Particle transport and energy flow are central for our understanding of a wealth of phenomena in physics and the natural sciences. Interactions are generically expected to promote ergodicity and diffusive behavior, yet quantum interference can arrest transport and prevent energy absorption, defying classical expectations. How interactions and quantum coherence compete remains a fundamental open question. Here, we experimentally investigate their interplay in a periodically driven, three-dimensional (3D) quantum gas with tunable interactions. Strikingly, we find that interactions give rise to a sharp dynamical boundary that separates localization from diffusive energy absorption. By tuning the driving amplitude and interaction strength, we map the localization-delocalization phase diagram and characterize the boundary via finite-time scaling. On the insulating side, we observe many-body dynamical localization (MBDL) for a wide range of parameters, finding arrested transport in momentum space. Near the boundary, transport becomes subdiffusive, whereas in the delocalized regime we observe classical diffusion, yielding a metal-insulator transition that we interpret in terms of localization in many-body Hilbert space. Our results exemplify an interaction-enabled dynamical phase transition in a closed Floquet many-body system and clarify how coherence and interactions jointly govern the quantum-to-classical transition.
summary: The researchers experimentally observed a metal-insulator phase transition in a 3D driven Bose-Einstein condensate. By tuning interactions and driving strength, they identified a sharp boundary between a localized phase (MBDL) and a diffusive phase. This work clarifies how interactions and quantum coherence jointly govern transport in isolated many-body systems.
main_problem: Understanding the competition between quantum interference, which promotes localization, and interactions, which promote ergodicity and diffusion, in a driven many-body system.
main_result: Discovery of an interaction-enabled metal-insulator phase transition in a 3D quantum gas, characterized by a sharp dynamical boundary between many-body dynamical localization and diffusive energy absorption.
method: Experimental implementation of a 3D Quantum Kicked Rotor using a Cesium BEC, combined with finite-time scaling analysis and interaction-quench tests to verify the transition's nature.
model_or_system: A 3D quantum gas consisting of approximately 10,000 133-Cs atoms in a Bose-Einstein Condensate, subject to a periodic optical lattice pulse protocol (Quantum Kicked Rotor) with tunable s-wave scattering via Feshbach resonance.
key_observables: Momentum distribution n(k), energy proxy E(Np) (second moment of momentum distribution), and spatial density distribution.
important_parameters: Kick strength (kappa), interaction strength (as), number of kicks (Np), and pulse period (T).
main_assumptions: The system is treated as a closed Floquet many-body system; the scaling analysis assumes an effective dimensionality of d=3.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental setup and momentum distributions; Fig 2 presents the localization-delocalization phase diagram; Fig 3 demonstrates the reversibility of the transition via interaction ramps; Fig 5 shows interaction quench effects on peak width; Fig 6 shows energy proxy evolution at the critical point.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of interaction vs. coherence, describes the 3D BEC experimental platform and QKR protocol, presents the mapping of the phase diagram and scaling analysis, demonstrates the reversibility of the transition, and concludes with the implications for the quantum-to-classical transition.
why_interesting: It provides a direct experimental realization of a dynamical phase transition in a many-body system, demonstrating how interactions can actively reorganize Hilbert space to enable transport, which is fundamental to understanding the emergence of classicality and ergodicity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig 1. Experimental setup and observation of localization and delocalization. a Schematics of the experimental setup (top) and of the protocol (bottom): The BEC (blue) confined in crossed trapping beams (green) is subjected to Np pulses of a vertically oriented optical lattice (orange) before undergoing ToF imaging or, alternatively, in-situ imaging. b, c, Momentum distribution n(k) plotted after applying a certain number of kicks Np for (b) κ = 0.75, as = 220 a0 and (c) κ = 1.5, as = 775 a0. d, e, Energy proxy E as a function of Np for 3 values of the scattering length as = 0 a0 (blue circles), 220 a0 (yellow circles), and 775 a0 (red circles) for (d) κ = 0.75 and (e) κ = 1.5. The dashed...

Fig 2. Metal-insulator phase diagram and finite-time scaling analysis. a,b, Phase diagrams constructed from the measured (a) and the numerically determined (b) energy proxy E(Np) after Np =500 kicks as a function of κ and as. c, Scaling function ln Λ as a function of ln[1/N 1/3 p ] for different values of κ as measured at as = 220 a0. d, Data collapse in (c) onto a universal scaling function, with ln Λ plotted against the scaled quantity ln[ξ(κ)/N1/3 k ]. e, Extracted scaling parameter ξ(κ) as a function of κ, fitted by a Lorentzian (solid black line). The critical value of κc =1.16 is indicated by the dotted line.

Fig 3. Reversibility of the MBDL-to-delocalization phase transition. Evolution of E versus Np at κ = 0.75 for as = 220 a0 (blue circles), 775 a0 (orange circles), and in- teraction ramp-up from 220 a0 to 775 a0 (purple dots) and ramp-down from 775 a0 to 220 a0 (green dots) at Np = 400. The dashed and dotted lines are linear fits. The insets provide a comparison of n(k) at Np = 800 (blue line) and Np = 1200 (red line) for the loss of MBDL (case A), top) and the regain- ing of MBDL (case B), bottom).

Fig 4. Real-space dynamics for many-body QKR. Time evolution of the spatial density ρ(z) for (a) the MBDL case with (κ, as/a0) = (0.75, 220), for (b) the delocalized case with (κ, as/a0)=(1.5,775), and for (c) the MBDL case for which the kicking is stopped at Np = 300. Insets: Numerical simulation results under the same conditions as the experiment. The red dashed lines in (b) and the dashed-dotted lines in (c) indicate linear expansion for a speed corresponding to ±6.5ℏkL and ±2ℏkL, respectively, as a guide to the eye.

Figure 5. Interaction quench. The width of the central peak σz is plotted as a function of hold time thold for ini- tial as = 220 a0 and κ = 0.75. The colored (grey) data are taken with (without) an interaction quench to as = 0 a0 af- ter Np kicks as indicated, ranging from zero to 1000. The dashed lines are sinusoidal fits to the oscillation data, giving 37.4(4) Hz after zero kicks and 36.3(3) Hz after 1000 kicks. For comparison, the trap frequency along the z-direction is determined to ωz/(2π) = 18.7(3) Hz via dipole-mode excita- tion measurements. The shaded areas reflect the standard deviation obtained from 3 repetitions.
